content: When a refine/inventory ticket asks to compare N surfaces against a new analytical lens (role-card shape, loop-moves, anatomy gaps), scope the lens to the surfaces where it carries semantic meaning (e.g. actual LLM prompts) rather than running it across every deterministic-script/config row too — the latter produces mostly-null cells and quadruples the document for no signal.

Do not author full new role-card/persona content as a side effect of a size:S inventory ticket — an inventory ticket's job is to identify candidates (source prompts + one-line rationale) and defer authoring to a follow-up implementation issue, especially when the pattern being adopted (e.g. agency-agents role cards) has zero prior references in the repo.

When a refinement-prompt ticket is asked to consolidate a baked persona-prompt bundle (refinement-skills/ -> /opt/refinement-skills/) toward a clone-live Claude Skill layout, do not bundle the physical directory rename + reference updates (Dockerfile/entrypoint.sh/scheduler.sh/context_budget.py/etc.) with a same-ticket prose de-duplication pass, even if a sibling policy spec designed the target layout already. The rename is a baked-image-to-clone-live delivery-mechanism migration (different risk class, needs its own Docker/CI verification) and should stay its own follow-up ticket; only trim/merge prose in place at the current path.

When an Archon phase command (clone-live, DAG-dispatched, e.g. dark-factory-refine.md) and a baked reference-skill prompt (e.g. refinement-skills/orchestrator-prompt.md) describe the same procedure in duplicate prose, the Archon command is always the canonical owner and the baked file shrinks to a thin persona stub - not the reverse. The dispatched, clone-live file already carries the real operational detail (scope boundary, memory rules, model pins, publish templates); do not migrate that detail into the baked file even if it originated there. Keep the stub file present (not deleted) if any script enumerates it by filename for token-budget accounting (e.g. context_budget.py's _SKILL_PROMPT_FILES).

Before declaring historical quantitative data (tokens/duration) 'not measurable,' check whether entrypoint.sh's post_cost_report() already posts a durable per-node cost-report comment (marker  dark-factory-cost-report ) to the issue — it does, since 2026-05-27, and is minable the same way conformance/code-review verdicts are mined from issue comments.

When a 'roll out X modularization' ticket in the Claude-Skills epic family depends on a prior evaluation ticket whose scorecard clears no scenario for default-on/blocking invocation, scope the rollout to documenting+runtime-testing the wrapper for scenarios that already have a live skill artifact (Tier 1) only; do not build a first skill-modularized alternative or anticipatory wrapper/alias layer for a scenario the scorecard rates 'advisory-readiness'/confounded (Tier 2) — that is net-new gate-adjacent production surface the eval didn't clear, and belongs to a future ticket once a skill body exists.

A rollout-status/runbook doc whose job is to record ongoing, mutable operational state (e.g. per-scenario tier/rollout state that changes as evals advance) should be authored as its own living spec kept permanently at docs/superpowers/specs/ (status line marked living, only the ticket's plan doc archived) rather than folded into an existing tested policy spec (couples churny operational state into a stable, content-asserting-test-guarded doc) or a brand-new top-level docs/ location (no repo precedent outside the docs/superpowers/specs convention for authored design/policy prose).

The added test pins the rollout runbook to `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-13-roll-out-dark-factory-claude-skills-design.md` and asserts it is "not archived", but a later commit on this same branch (`e771def docs: archive spec/plan for issue #49`) moved that file into `docs/archive/`. 5 of these tests now fail against HEAD, so `python -m pytest tests/` (exactly what CI runs) goes red and blocks the PR. This is the CLAUDE.md "never archive a doc that tests or README still reference" rule being violated. Fix by restoring the spec to `docs/superpowers/specs/` (revert the archive move) — not by loosening the test.

When adding an HTTP-calling Tracker/CodeHost provider adapter (e.g. Jira, GitLab), use stdlib urllib.request behind a single internal request-helper seam -- do not add requests/httpx/vcrpy/responses. This repo has zero third-party HTTP or HTTP-mocking dependencies (no requirements.txt/pyproject.toml; Dockerfile pip-installs only codeindex/pre-commit/pyyaml); every existing HTTP call in scripts/factory_core/ already uses urllib.request. 'Recorded HTTP fixtures' in this repo means hand-rolled JSON fed through a monkeypatched request-helper (mirroring test_provider_tracker_parity.py's subprocess.run monkeypatch idiom), not a VCR cassette library.

The provider-abstraction design doc's 'shared Tracker/CodeHost contract suite' (docs/provider-abstraction-design.md section 10) does not exist yet as of the #251 refinement pass -- only an ABC-shape test (test_provider_tracker_base.py) and GitHub-only golden-argv parity tests exist. An adapter ticket whose acceptance criteria says 'passes the same contract suite as GitHubTracker' must itself author the missing shared, implementation-agnostic behavioral suite (parametrized over both implementations via each one's own I/O-monkeypatch seam), not just add its own fixture tests -- the narrower per-implementation reading under-delivers the acceptance criterion.

When a ticket 'Depends on' a sibling provider-abstraction step whose PR already exists but is stuck on needs-discussion and unmerged (e.g. #250's registry/_TRACKERS wiring), do not assume it will have landed by implementation time. Build the new adapter standalone against the current (pre-dependency) ABC shape on main, implement any forward-looking hook (e.g. required_env()) directly on the new class without touching the shared registry file, and defer wiring to the blocked ticket or a trivial follow-up -- touching the registry/ABC yourself collides with the in-flight PR and violates scope discipline.

Tracker.get_children()'s only real shape precedent is GitHubTracker's raw GraphQL envelope (state: OPEN/CLOSED, labels: {nodes:[{name}]}), silently baked into epic_autopilot.py's _sub_issue_numbers (its only caller, disabled by default). New Tracker adapters should return the adapter-neutral shape ({id, status: canonical, labels: [str]}) matching get_item()'s conventions, not fabricate GitHub's envelope -- epic_autopilot.py's GitHub-shape coupling is a known, separately-tracked gap (harmless while epic_autopilot stays off by default), not something new adapters should perpetuate.

For session-window/rate-limit exhaustion tickets, scope detection to reactive signals only (structured claude.rate_limit_event/resetsAt in run output, falling back to the existing entrypoint.sh substring heuristic) — do not attempt proactive polling of api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage for utilization-based holding. The factory's CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN lacks the required user:profile scope, and granting it is a credential/deploy-surface change (human-only); a ticket that gates on it will strand

When factory_wip_limit is low (e.g. 1), do not handle a shared-resource exhaustion signature (e.g. Claude session-window limit) with an in-container sleep-and-retry loop — sleeping inside the container holds the sole WIP slot hostage for the whole wait and is invisible to the scheduler. Instead write a resume-epoch sentinel file to SCHEDULER_STATE_DIR (mirroring the main-is-red pattern) and exit immediately (exit 0, bypassing on_failure) so the scheduler gates ALL dispatch priorities on the sentinel; no new per-call-site breaker logic is needed since dispatch-skip alone prevents any retry-count increment

When adding a regression test for a single scheduler.sh helper function (e.g. a pure jq transform like get_items_by_status), extend the existing tests/test_scheduler.sh as a new section (it already provides SCHEDULER_SOURCE_ONLY=1 source scheduler.sh + assert_eq + gh/docker/python3 stubs) rather than creating a new standalone test_scheduler_<x>.sh file. tests/test_scheduler_ceiling.sh is a structural (awk/grep source-text) test, not a behavioral one, and tests/test_scheduler_pagination.sh duplicates section O's in-file coverage while re-implementing ~30-60 lines of stub/source boilerplate for one function — a standalone file is only justified when the concern is genuinely large enough to need its own preamble.

When gating a DAG push/label node (e.g. refine-push, plan-push-and-advance) on whether an agent phase actually produced its artifact, check the committed file on the branch (git rev-list --count main..HEAD > 0 AND a tracked spec/plan file referencing the issue), not the ephemeral $ARTIFACTS_DIR/refinement-status.md STATUS marker, and not an OR of both. The status file is written to the run's artifacts dir (never committed) and only proves agent intent, not a reviewable artifact — it could exist even if the commit itself failed. Rejected: OR-ing the two signals, because that re-opens the exact stranding hole being fixed (label applied with nothing to review).

DAG bash: nodes in workflows/archon-dark-factory.yaml are separate script invocations that do NOT source entrypoint.sh — they cannot call its post_or_update_comment() helper or reference REFINE_FAILURE_MARKER directly. For idempotent marker-based issue comments from within a DAG bash node, call scripts/factory_core/providers/cli.py's 'tracker comment --id <n> --marker <m> --body-file <f>' directly (the same primitive post_or_update_comment wraps) rather than re-implementing upsert logic or falling back to a bare one-shot 'gh issue comment' (fine for genuinely terminal one-off failures like preview_fail, but produces duplicate comments across scheduler retries for anything gated by REFINE_MAX_RETRIES).

When refining a memory-backend/idea-source evaluation spike (e.g. Mem0 #50), scope Part-B-style 'improve the system' follow-up recommendations only to genuine gaps found by auditing current codebase state (Phase 3 context assembly), not to every idea in the issue's original list — several ideas (hybrid retrieval scoring, role-scoped retrieval, markdown-as-source-of-truth, eval suite) were already shipped in scripts/memory_retrieve.py and eval_memory_quality.py by the time #50 was refined; recommending 'new' tickets for shipped work is itself a scope-discipline violation and wastes a future refine/plan/implement cycle rediscovering existing code.

When spiking a new candidate memory/data-backend for Dark Factory, reuse the four operational-footprint criteria from the agentmemory spike (#644/#661, verdict do-not-adopt) as named, directly-comparable benchmark rows: install/deploy footprint, role/path filter support, retrieval latency at the factory's actual corpus scale (tens of entries, not thousands), and state durability across container restarts. These are candidate-agnostic operational requirements the factory needs from any backend, not agentmemory-specific quirks, so re-deriving them (not copy-pasting agentmemory's specific numbers) against each new candidate keeps verdicts comparable across spikes.

A prior spike's merge commit message can claim deliverables (e.g. #661's message claimed a spec file at docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-27-agentmemory-memory-backend-spike.md and a .archon/memory AVOID entry) that were never actually committed to the repo — only scripts/eval_agentmemory.sh and scripts/iii-config.agentmemory.yaml survived from that spike. Before citing a prior ticket's outcome as precedent, verify claimed artifacts actually exist (git show --stat <commit>, grep the working tree) rather than trusting the commit message's stated scope.

Do not adopt Mem0 (mem0ai) as a Dark Factory memory backend as of the #50 spike — verdict 'idea-only' on FAIL_COUNT=0/6 operational rows (see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-17-mem0-memory-v2-spike-design.md Live Evaluation Results for the full table). Retrieval-quality/design ideas from the spike (append-only events, entity extraction, procedural handoff memory, retrieval explanations) remain valid follow-ups independent of this verdict.

The adapter loader (factory_core/adapter.py) stays dependency-free — no jsonschema or other schema library, even for structured blocks like loops:. The Dockerfile pins only pyyaml for this tooling (comment: avoid transitive-dep reliance before target deps install); hand-rolled isinstance/AdapterError validation was chosen over jsonschema for the new loops: schema (issue #195) and should stay the pattern for future adapter schema additions (A2-A5, epic #194) unless the shape becomes too large for hand-rolling to stay legible.

When adding a new validated block to .factory/adapter.yaml, scope any new strict/unknown-key-is-error validation to ONLY the new block's own entries — leave existing blocks (safety, components, memory_routing, deconflict, token_optimization) on their legacy warn-and-carry behavior. The 'v1 adapter / absent adapter behaves byte-identically to today' acceptance bar is a hard constraint that existing parity tests do not fully cover (they never inject a stray key inside a known block), so retroactively tightening a pre-existing block is a real regression risk even though tests would stay green. Decided during refinement of issue #195 (loops: block, epic #194 A1); applies to A2-A5 if they add further adapter blocks.

schema_version in .factory/adapter.yaml is inert free-form-int metadata — adapter.py only ever checks isinstance(int), it never branches parsing/validation logic on the declared value. Do not make schema_version a feature gate (e.g. rejecting loops: under schema_version 1); new blocks should validate whenever the key is present in the YAML, independent of schema_version, to avoid inventing version-conditional parsing the byte-identical-parity requirement does not ask for. Decided during refinement of issue #195 (epic #194 A1) and relevant to any future adapter schema bump.

factory_core Python modules that need a .factory/adapter.yaml value should import adapter directly and call adapter.get(clone_dir, 'dotted.key') with a fail-open try/except around it (pattern already used by deconflict.py, main_red_fixer.py, epic_autopilot.py) — not have entrypoint.sh shell-read the adapter and pass JSON through a flag. The shell-passthrough pattern in gate_lib.sh exists only because that caller is pure shell with no Python module to host the read. Confirmed during refinement of issue #195 when wiring run_record.py to surface loops: for provenance; relevant to any future factory_core module that needs adapter config.

When a research/comment-channel-sourced proposal would substantially grow an already-spec'd ticket's schema/validation/test surface past its size label, do not fold it in directly and do not silently drop it: ship a minimal reserved-key-rejection carve-out (hard AdapterError naming the tracking epic/ticket) for the proposed names now, and file the substantive extension as an immediate follow-up ticket reusing the source brief as its content. Applied during refinement of issue #195/epic #194 A1 (adapter loops: schema) when five Hermes-Agent-sourced research proposals (role_card, five-move restructuring, skills, economics, memory_intervention/mechanism_candidates) arrived after the spec was already written; the reserved-name carve-out for memory_intervention (epic #241) and mechanism_candidates shipped in A1 itself, the rest deferred to a new 'A1.5' ticket.

When an issue comment claims an out-of-band authorization now exists elsewhere (e.g. 'CLAUDE.md was updated on main, verify in your clone'), verify the claim directly (git log/show against origin/main, gh pr view) before acting on it rather than trusting the comment's assertion, even when it cites specific PR numbers/commit SHAs. Confirmed during refinement of issue #195: a chain of comments claimed escalating authorization for an initially-refused, security-sensitive schema proposal (tool allow/deny-list fields); the final claim (PR #299 merged into CLAUDE.md sanctioning a comment channel) turned out to be genuinely true on independent verification, but should never be accepted on the strength of the comment alone since the same channel had already been correctly flagged as untrusted once.

When a Hermes-Agent-authored issue proposes a Done-transition-blocking 'completion contract'/verifier system bundled into a regression-repair ticket (e.g. #300's cost-report fix), scope the ticket to the repair + fail-loud diagnostics + durable sink only, even if the issue's own backlog-ownership section explicitly defers the generalized verifier/gate work to separate tickets (#197/#198 in this case) — building gate-blocking behavior under the repair ticket violates CLAUDE.md's 'gate changes get their own reviewed ticket' hard limit regardless of the proposing comment's trusted signature

When a parsed value must be bounded by a physical/product invariant (e.g. Claude Max's fixed 5h session window in session_window.py's resume-epoch clamp), hardcode the bound as a module constant rather than a config.yaml key, even though sibling values in the same function (buffer_minutes, fallback_minutes) are configurable. Those are operator policy knobs; a physical-invariant bound made configurable could be set back to a value that reopens the exact bug it exists to prevent — keep it a code-reviewed constant.

session_window.py's parse_fallback_reset_epoch, when a bare wall-clock reset time has already passed today, must return today's literal (past) timestamp, not now_epoch and not tomorrow. Rejected: rolling forward a day (the #305 bug, ~22h false pause) and substituting now_epoch (manufactures certainty the parser doesn't have and duplicates resume-policy logic that belongs in compute_resume_epoch's buffer and scheduler.sh's self-clearing now>=resume_epoch gate). Keep the parser single-purpose; let the existing downstream layers turn a past return value into an effectively-immediate resume.

Before extracting entrypoint.sh's rate-limit reset-time parsing logic (e.g. for #182), verify it hasn't already shipped: it moved to scripts/factory_core/session_window.py via #35/#305 and entrypoint.sh no longer contains it. A discarded 2026-07-07 spec for #182 listed it as a third deliverable using stale line numbers; the 2026-07-22 re-refinement re-derived entrypoint.sh's current line numbers from main (grew 784->1005 lines across 26 commits) rather than trusting the issue body or a stale prior spec. Always re-verify line-number citations in an issue body/prior spec against current main before planning work on a long-lived entrypoint.sh ticket.

When extracting a bash function into factory_core + cli.py where the full rendered output depends on a network fetch (e.g. post_cost_report's prior-comment gh api call) that itself must only happen when there's something to post, don't collapse the seam into one 'render' subcommand that both decides renderability and produces output. Split into a cheap guard subcommand (e.g. cost-report check: pure local-data check, exits non-zero + stderr diagnostic on empty, dispatches any best-effort telemetry in-process) that bash calls before any network I/O, and a separate producer subcommand (cost-report render) that bash only calls after the guard succeeds and after doing its own gh fetch. Rejected during #182 refinement: a single subcommand that 'always prints full body on success', because it cannot avoid an unconditional gh call on the empty-rows path while also consuming the prior-comment-body argument.

When collapsing scheduler.sh's 3x duplicated MAIN_IS_RED skip-guard (P1.5/P2/P3) into one declarative dispatch-table guard, do not apply the resulting guard uniformly to every dispatch-capable stage: P1 (in-review comment triage) has no MAIN_IS_RED/SESSION_WINDOW_PAUSED guard at all today, and P4/P5 (plan/refine) are gated by SESSION_WINDOW_PAUSED only, not MAIN_IS_RED. A uniform guard is a real behavior change (freezing stages that currently keep running under red-main/paused conditions), not a pure internal-seam refactor. Verify each stage's actual current guard condition individually before consolidating.

scheduler.sh's predicate-function region (~L236-443) mixes true side-effect-free predicates with three 'check' functions (spec_advance_check, plan_advance_check, end_gate_check) that call dispatch()/set_board_status()/gh issue comment. When extracting predicates into a sourceable lib (mirroring scripts/gate_lib.sh's no-side-effects shape), only move the pure ones; the three impure check functions are micro-stages, not predicates, and belong in scheduler.sh itself — moving them would smuggle dispatch machinery into a file meant to have none.
